text: The surgeon uses a monopolar crown in coagulating mode to open the ovarian cortex of the right ovary, while the left ovary appears normal.

The surgeon opens the ovarian cortex using a monopolar crown, initiating the cystectomy with traction and counter traction. A small amount of energy is applied briefly to the ovary, followed by dissection using two forceps.

The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to increase the dissecting plane between the healthy ovary and the ovarian cyst, carefully continuing dissection on the opposite side to avoid injuring the dermoid cyst. The goal is to remove the entire cyst without opening it.

The surgeon continues the dissection of the ovarian dermoid cyst using traction and counter traction, applying small amounts of energy to facilitate removal from its bed. The cyst is progressively being separated, with minimal energy use to preserve ovarian follicles. Mechanical hemostasis is performed during the procedure.
The surgeon uses operative forceps to detach the final attachment of the ovary from the cyst, completing the cystectomy. The cyst is removed safely, ensuring no harm to the residual ovarian tissue.
The surgeon performs hemostasis on the residual ovary using a small stitch, avoiding the use of bipolar coagulation, while managing a large six-centimeter dermoid cyst.
The surgeon uses a two monocryl filament to restore the normal anatomy, carefully placing the stitch without tying it on the ovary to minimize the risk of ovarian ischemia.

The surgeon applies a stitch to achieve an anatomically favorable result, using an intra-corporeal knotting technique. The knot is carefully placed on the ovary without tying it, minimizing the risk of ovarian ischemia.

The surgeon demonstrates the anatomical result after cystectomy, cutting the excess stitch. A classical handbag and a twelve millimeter draw card are used for protective ovarian cyst extraction. The dermoid cyst is placed inside the handbag and extracted from the left iliac trochanter.
The surgeon empties the cyst transabdominally into the protective handbag, ensuring safe extraction. The emptied cyst is visible inside the handbag, showing the final aspect of the surgery.
